1: Ps 111: Acrostic Praise of YHWH's Covenant Works — Companion to Ps 112. Ps 111 is an acrostic psalm: each of the 22 half-lines begins with successive Hebrew letters (Aleph to Taw). The structural correspondence with Ps 112 is intentional — Ps 111 praises YHWH's works; Ps 112 (identical structure, same vocabulary) praises the one who fears YHWH, showing that the character of YHWH is reproduced in those who fear him. Together they form a diptych. 'I will give thanks to the LORD with my whole heart, in the company of the upright, in the congregation' (odeh YHWH bechol levav besod yesharim veaedah) — the praise is explicitly corporate: in the 'council of the upright' (sod yesharim, the intimate circle of the righteous) and in the 'congregation' (edah, the assembly). Private devotion leads to public praise.
2: 'Great are the works of the LORD, studied by all who delight in them' — 'great are the works' (gedolim ma'asei YHWH) — the theology of YHWH's works as the primary object of study. 'Studied by all who delight in them' (derushim lechol chephtzeihem) — the verb darash (to seek, inquire, study diligently) is the same root used for 'seeking YHWH' in covenant literature. Delighting in YHWH's works drives their study; the works reward study with deeper delight. This is the epistemology of worship: observation and reflection on what YHWH has done creates the material for praise.
3: 'Full of splendor and majesty is his work, and his righteousness endures forever' — 'splendor and majesty' (hod vehadar) — the royal vocabulary of divine glory (Psalms 96:6; Psalms 104:1; Psalms 145:5). 'His righteousness endures forever' (vetzidqato omedet laad) — the first occurrence of this phrase, which appears again at vv9 and 10, and is borrowed for the righteous man of Psalms 112:3,9 (2 Corinthians 9:9 quotes Psalms 112:9 in the context of generous giving). The enduring tzedaqah (righteousness) is both God's character and the character imparted to those who fear him.
4: 'He has caused his wondrous works to be remembered; the LORD is gracious and merciful' — 'caused his wondrous works to be remembered' (zeker asah leniphleotav) — YHWH has established a memorial (zeker) for his wondrous works. The Passover and the Sabbath are divine institutions for making the covenant acts memorable. 'Gracious and merciful' (channun verachum YHWH) — the divine self-description from Exodus 34:6-7 (the 'Thirteen Attributes': 'the LORD, the LORD, a God merciful and gracious...'). The liturgical formula is quoted throughout the Psalter and prophets to ground petitions in YHWH's revealed character.
5: 'He provides food for those who fear him; he remembers his covenant forever' — 'provides food' (teref natan liyre'av — teref, prey/food, the word for the hunter's catch) — divine provision framed in hunting imagery: YHWH provides the food that his covenant people need. 'Remembers his covenant' (yizkor leolam berito) — covenant memory as the ground of provision. YHWH's provision of manna in the wilderness (Exod 16), of food in the land, and of the sacrificial system's covenant meal are all expressions of this covenantal feeding.
6: 'He has shown his people the power of his works, in giving them the inheritance of the nations' — 'the power of his works' (koach ma'asav) — the power demonstrated in the works is the basis of trust for the future. 'Inheritance of the nations' (nachalat goyim) — the Conquest: YHWH demonstrated his power specifically by displacing the Canaanite nations and giving their land to Israel as an inheritance (nachalah). The Conquest is the primary OT demonstration of divine power after the Exodus.
7: 'The works of his hands are faithful and just; all his precepts are trustworthy' — 'faithful and just' (emet umishpat) — the works are characterized by truth (emet) and justice (mishpat): the same qualities demanded of human judges (Leviticus 19:15; Deuteronomy 16:20). 'His precepts are trustworthy' (piqqudav ne'emanim) — the torah's commands (piqqudim) partake of the same reliability as YHWH's works. What YHWH does and what YHWH commands are both equally dependable.
8: 'They are established forever and ever, to be performed with faithfulness and uprightness' — 'established forever and ever' (semuchim laad leolam) — permanence: the divine precepts and works are not time-bound but eternal. 'Performed with faithfulness and uprightness' (asuyim be-emet veyashar) — YHWH's commands are done in truth and straightforwardness. The divine character guarantees both the permanence of the covenant and the reliability of its terms.
9: 'He sent redemption to his people; he has commanded his covenant forever. Holy and awesome is his name!' — 'sent redemption' (shalach geulah leamo) — the Exodus as the paradigmatic act of redemption. 'His covenant forever' (berito leolam) — the covenant established at Sinai (and ultimately rooted in the Abrahamic covenant) has permanent validity. 'Holy and awesome is his name' (qadosh venora shemo) — the Sinai revelation (Exod 19-20) established YHWH's holiness and the awe it demands. The Name as holy is the reason the Third Commandment prohibits its misuse.
10: 'The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom; a good understanding have all those who practice it. His praise endures forever!' — 'the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom' (reshit chochmah yir'at YHWH) — the climax of the acrostic and its most famous line; essentially identical with Proverbs 9:10 ('The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom, and knowledge of the Holy One is understanding'). The reshit (beginning/chief part) of wisdom is not an introductory step to be transcended but the foundational orientation of all human knowing. Wisdom that does not begin with covenant reverence is not biblical wisdom. The acrostic closes with the summary principle that organizes all the preceding praise.
